completeness.rationale: All major expected extracts were captured: memory growth pattern (~500MB to 4GB+), v2.3 upgrade timing (~1 week ago), ~200 active users, daily consistency, restart workaround, and 8GB RAM VM specs. The v2.3 changelog detail about notification changes was surfaced indirectly — the reporter volunteered that the notification system was 'improved in v2.3' without being explicitly prompted to consult the changelog, so the underlying signal was captured but not as precisely as it could have been.

accuracy.rationale: The summary correctly identifies the v2.3 upgrade as the trigger and the WebSocket notification subsystem as the source of the leak. Crucially, the recommended fix explicitly calls out 'event listener lifecycle' and 'uncleared listener arrays' as prime suspects — matching the actual root cause precisely. This satisfies the first acceptable diagnostic path and closely approximates the canonical mechanism without having a heap dump in hand.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent systematically ruled out alternatives: database connection leaks, time-based accumulation vs activity-based, connection-level leaks vs per-notification accumulation, and third-party plugins. It then proposed a confirmatory test (disabling real-time notifications) and requested a diagnostic artifact (heap dump). No obvious follow-up angles were left unexplored given the information available.

economy.rationale: Four turns, each building meaningfully on the previous answers. The database/connection pooling question in turn one was somewhat speculative given the reporter had already localized the issue to the application server process, but connection pool leaks are a common memory leak cause and worth ruling out. All other questions were well-targeted and non-redundant.

question_quality.rationale: The activity-vs-time-based question was particularly insightful — it elegantly distinguished per-connection accumulation from per-notification accumulation, which directly shaped the root cause hypothesis in a way the reporter would not have volunteered unprompted. The follow-up on WebSocket connection count further refined the diagnosis. The notification-disable experiment was a well-designed confirmation test rather than a generic debugging step.

actionability.rationale: The summary gives a developer an immediate starting point: diff the notification subsystem between v2.2 and v2.3, focus on event listener lifecycle and objects retained after notification delivery, and use the incoming heap dump to pinpoint the exact type. Reproduction steps, an immediate workaround, a proposed test case, and a regression comparison approach are all included. A developer could begin coding a fix from this summary.

notable_strengths: Surfaced the v2.3 upgrade as the likely trigger in the very first turn by asking about version — the reporter hadn't connected the upgrade to the issue before being asked
The activity-correlation question elegantly distinguished per-connection leaks from per-notification accumulation, directly informing the root cause hypothesis
Proposed a concrete, production-safe confirmation test (disabling real-time notifications) that doubles as a workaround
Specifically named 'event listener registrations' and 'uncleared listener arrays' in the recommended fix, matching the actual root cause mechanism
notable_weaknesses: Did not explicitly prompt the reporter to read the v2.3 changelog for specifics on what changed in the notification subsystem — doing so could have surfaced the 'improved real-time notifications' entry and strengthened the hypothesis earlier
The database connection pooling question was somewhat off-track given the reporter had already isolated high memory to the application server process specifically
most_insightful_question: Does the memory climb track with user activity, or is it purely time-based? For example, on weekends or lighter-traffic days, does memory still climb at the same rate, or does it grow more slowly?
missed_opportunities: Asking the reporter to check the v2.3 release notes or changelog for the exact description of what changed in the notification system — would have corroborated the hypothesis with first-party evidence

Estimating the expected per-request memory overhead from user count and request volume to cross-validate against the observed growth rate — would have either strengthened or challenged the per-notification leak hypothesis quantitatively
